title: To Miss Cruikshank, A Very Young Lady. Written On The Blank Leaf Of A Book, Presented To Her By The Author.
content:
Beauteous rose-bud, young and gay,
        Blooming in thy early May,
        Never may'st thou, lovely flow'r,
        Chilly shrink in sleety show'r!
        Never Boreas' hoary path,
        Never Eurus' poisonous breath,
        Never baleful stellar lights,
        Taint thee with untimely blights!
        Never, never reptile thief
        Riot on thy virgin leaf!
        Nor even Sol too fiercely view
        Thy bosom blushing still with dew!

        May'st thou long, sweet crimson gem,
        Richly deck thy native stem:
        'Till some evening, sober, calm,
        Dropping dews and breathing balm,
        While all around the woodland rings,
        And ev'ry bird thy requiem sings;
        Thou, amid the dirgeful sound,
        Shed thy dying honours round,
        And resign to parent earth
        The loveliest form she e'er gave birth.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Burns represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Burns's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
